name: Virgil and the Aeneas Compiler
summary: Ben Titzer's Virgil (compiled by Aeneas, 2006+) is the compiler-engineering existence proof for production-grade whole-program optimisation with ~50× faster compilation than rustc and ~300× faster than TinyGo, plus binary outputs ~35× smaller than Rust and ~20× smaller than TinyGo on benchmarks.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Ben Titzer's **Virgil** (compiled by **Aeneas**, the Virgil compiler binary, 2006–present) is a compiler-engineering existence proof: production-grade whole-program optimisation with compile times and binary sizes one to two orders of magnitude better than mainstream alternatives. Status (as of 2026-04): public measurements (linked from `github.com/titzer/virgil/issues/80`) report Virgil compiling benchmarks ~50× faster than rustc and ~300× faster than TinyGo, with executable output ~35× smaller than Rust and ~20× smaller than TinyGo; numbers are upstream-published, not independently re-benchmarked here.

sources.label: Virgil — A Fast and Lightweight Systems Programming Language

sections.title: Techniques that get Virgil there
sections.content:
- **Reachability-first compilation.** The compiler parses and type-checks all code but only lowers reachable code from `main()`. Unreachable generic code never gets specialised, imported-but-unused modules never get codegened. Most languages do dead-code elimination post-optimisation; Virgil does it pre-optimisation, saving the optimiser's time on code that will be discarded.
- **Specialisation driven by reachability.** Polymorphic instantiation only happens for combinations that are actually reached — not the Cartesian product of all type arguments × all call sites. This collapses the generic explosion that hurts compile times in Rust and C++.
- **Local SSA optimisations, no fixed-point iteration.** Most of Virgil's optimiser is lightweight local peephole-style rewrites on SSA. Unlike LLVM, which iterates many passes to saturation, Virgil does mostly single-pass transformations. Less aggressive, much faster.
- **Fully self-hosted, no runtime in another language.** Compiler, runtime, GC, libraries, system interface are all in Virgil. On native targets, there is no C startup code — Virgil's own `_start` calls directly into the kernel.
- **Multi-target from one compiler.** Same source compiles to x86-darwin, x86-64-linux, JAR (JVM), or Wasm. The compiler is contained in a single executable; no separate linker, assembler, or runtime package.

sections.title: Design lesson
sections.content:
The lesson for a new language: "LLVM-level optimisation" is not always the right target. For systems where compile latency is a first-class concern (game engines, dev workflows, small embedded targets), a simpler compiler with reachability analysis can deliver both faster builds and smaller binaries than a fully-optimising toolchain. The trade-off is that generated code is slower than LLVM -O3 output — but often only by a small margin on the workloads Virgil targets.

Virgil also publishes research on novel representations (Teo & Titzer, "Unboxing Virgil ADTs for Fun and Profit," JENSFEST 2024) demonstrating that whole-program knowledge enables ADT representation choices that would be unsafe under separate compilation.

sources.label: Why is Virgil so fast? (GitHub issue)
